meta.title: L494 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Range Rover Sport L494 engine — from the 2.0 Ingenium diesel to the supercharged V6 and SVR V8. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 12 vetted Land Rover specialists today.

@graph.name: Range Rover Sport L494 Engine Codes
Range Rover Sport L494 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Sport L494 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned L494 engine costs between £3,000 and £9,500 supply-only, depending on the engine — the 2.0 Ingenium diesel ranges £3,500–£5,800, while the 3.0 V6 diesel runs £6,500–£9,500. Fitting adds 10–20 hours of labour at £65–£90/hr. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Range Rover Sport L494 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 448DT 4.4-litre SDV8 is the most reliable diesel — no catastrophic design flaw, no crankshaft bearing failure like the 306DT, and a documented 200,000-mile potential. On petrol, the PT204 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 is the simplest and most dependable, with no supercharger cooling complexity.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Sport L494?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crankshaft bearing failure on the 3.0 V6 diesel (306DT), timing chain tensioner failure on pre-2019 Ingenium 2.0 diesels, water pump and coolant leaks on the supercharged V6 (306PS) and V8 (508PS), and oil cooler leaks on the 4.4 V8 diesel (448DT). The 306DT V6 diesel is the single biggest risk in the L494 range.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Sport L494 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine. The 306DT V6 diesel carries the highest risk of catastrophic failure. The 448DT V8 diesel is significantly more robust. The 2.0 Ingenium diesel (pre-2019) has timing chain tensioner concerns; post-2019 units are improved. Petrol options generally fare better, with the 2.0 P300 being the most dependable overall.

mainEntity.name: Which Range Rover Sport L494 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 306DT 3.0 V6 diesel without a documented service history and a cold-start knock inspection. We consistently advise buyers to steer clear of early 2.0 Ingenium diesels (pre-2019) without evidence of timing-chain replacement. If you must have a diesel, choose the 448DT 4.4 SDV8 or a post-2019 D300 Ingenium six-cylinder.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my L494 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (timing chain on diesel Ingenium), knocking on startup (V6 diesel bearing failure), persistent coolant loss or overheating (supercharged V6/V8), or blue smoke and rough idle on any high-mileage unit are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport L494 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Land Rover scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £18,000 L494, a £6,500 306DT recon is 36% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £10,000 early example, the same recon is 65% — borderline. For sub-£8,000 examples, we'd recommend walking away.

mainEntity.name: How long do Range Rover Sport L494 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 448DT 4.4 SDV8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — it's the most durable diesel in the range. The 306DT V6 diesel is capable of 150,000+ miles but requires scrupulous oil changes (5,000-mile intervals) to avoid bearing failure. The 2.0 Ingenium petrol can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles with routine maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the L494 and other Range Rover Sport generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The L494 is the second-generation Range Rover Sport, built from 2013 to 2022. It replaced the L320 (2005–2013) and was succeeded by the L461 in 2022. The L494 shares its aluminium D7u platform with the Range Rover L405, offering significantly lighter weight and better on-road dynamics than the previous generation.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Sport L494 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate, vehicle documentation, and often on a sticker in the driver's door jamb. For diesels, look for 306DT (TDV6/SDV6), 448DT (SDV8), or AJ20D6/DT306 (D300/D250). For petrols, look for PT204 (P300/P400e), 306PS (SCV6), or 508PS (V8 Supercharged/SVR). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll confirm.

hero.h1: Range Rover Sport L494 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Range Rover Sport L494 — from the entry-level 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel to the fire-breathing 5.0-litre supercharged V8 SVR. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the V6 diesel crankshaft issue that defines this generation's ownership experience. Backed by 308+ real UK L494 enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Range Rover Sport L494 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Range Rover Sport L494 specifically — from launch in 2013 through the 2018 facelift and the introduction of the SVR and PHEV variants.

overview.intro: The Range Rover Sport L494 is the second generation of Land Rover's performance-oriented SUV, launched in 2013 as the successor to the L320. Built on the aluminium D7u platform shared with the Range Rover L405, the L494 offered significant weight savings and improved dynamics over its predecessor. The engine line-up expanded dramatically over its nine-year production run: early cars offered 3.0-litre V6 diesel, 3.0-litre supercharged V6 petrol, and 5.0-litre supercharged V8 petrol options; a 2016 update introduced the 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel four; the 2018 facelift brought the 2.0-litre Ingenium petrol (P300), the P400e plug-in hybrid, and later the 3.0-litre Ingenium six-cylinder diesel MHEV (D300). The range-topping SVR delivered 575 bhp from its supercharged 5.0-litre V8.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Aluminium D7u (shared with Range Rover L405) • Predecessor: Range Rover Sport L320 (2005–2013) • Successor: Range Rover Sport L461 (2022–present) • Sibling body styles: None — L494 is sole second-generation Range Rover Sport body style
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Range Rover Sport L494 generated 308+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], with the 3.0-litre V6 diesel (306DT) accounting for the largest share of JLR-wide enquiries (980), followed by the 5.0-litre supercharged V8 (508PS) at 620 enquiries.

items.engine: 448DT 4.4-litre SDV8 — The 4.4-litre V8 diesel (Ford-sourced) is the most robust diesel in the L494 line-up. No catastrophic bottom-end design flaw, no crankshaft bearing failure like the 306DT, and oil cooler leaks are the primary concern — a manageable repair rather than an engine-killer.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless V8 torque without the V6 diesel's reliability anxiety.

items.engine: PT204 2.0-litre Ingenium P300 — The 300 bhp four-cylinder petrol is the most reliable petrol in the L494 line-up. No supercharger cooling system to manage, no V8 timing-chain complexity, and a simpler overall architecture than the V6 and V8 options.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol reliability without the V6/V8's fuel consumption.

items.engine: 306DT 3.0-litre V6 Diesel — The AJD V6 diesel suffers from crankshaft bearing failure (tin-aluminium coating fatigue) at 80,000–140,000 miles, often preceded by cold-start knock. A bottom-end rebuild costs £5,500–£7,000; a full reconditioned replacement runs £6,500–£9,500. This is the defining risk of the L494 generation.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with full service history, 5,000-mile oil changes, and a comprehensive warranty.

items.engine: 508PS 5.0-litre Supercharged V8 (SVR) — The SVR's 575 bhp V8 is the most expensive engine in the L494 line-up to replace, with reconditioned units costing £5,500–£9,000 supply-only. Timing-chain guide wear and cooling system leaks are the primary risks.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts and collectors who accept the premium.

items.engine: 204DTA 2.0-litre Ingenium SD4 — The 240 bhp diesel four offers the best balance of performance, economy, and running costs. Post-2019 units are significantly more reliable than the V6 diesel, and the recon cost is substantially lower than the 306DT.
items.whoItsFor: the majority of L494 buyers who want affordable running costs.

items.engine: 448DT 4.4-litre SDV8 — Often ignored by buyers who default to the V6 diesel or petrol V8, the 4.4 V8 diesel delivers effortless torque, excellent refinement, and significantly better reliability than the 306DT. It's the diesel that L494 buyers should be considering.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy with V8 refinement.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Range Rover Sport L494 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — pre-2019 units: budget for chain work
Too Early — no major pattern yet
Best Diesel — no catastrophic design flaw
Solid Choice — cooling system is the watch-item

economicsRule.title: The L494 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,500–£9,500 306DT recon against a £10,000–£14,000 early L494 sits at 46–95% of the car's value. On a sub-£12,000 L494, that brushes our 60% Land Rover threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £18,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 36–53% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L494 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: PT204 2.0-litre petrol — cheapest recon, simplest architecture.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L494 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 306DT V6 diesel — crankshaft bearing failure is catastrophic and expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L494 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 448DT 4.4-litre SDV8 — V8 refinement without the V6 diesel's catastrophic risk.

problems.title: 306DT V6 Diesel Crankshaft Bearing Failure
problems.affectedModels: L494 3.0-litre TDV6 and SDV6 (306DT), all years.
problems.rootCause: The tin-aluminium coating on the crankshaft bearings fatigues over time, leading to bearing failure. Cold-start knock is the classic early warning; ignored, it causes catastrophic bottom-end failure. The 306DT is the single biggest reliability risk in the L494 line-up.
problems.recommendation: If your L494 306DT exhibits cold-start knock, stop driving and have the bottom end inspected immediately — £5,500–£7,000 now is far cheaper than the £6,500–£9,500 replacement that follows a full failure.

cta.label: Compare 306DT engine prices for your Range Rover Sport →

problems.title: Ingenium 2.0 Diesel Timing Chain Tensioner Failure
problems.rootCause: The timing chain tensioner on early Ingenium 2.0-litre diesel engines can fail, leading to chain stretch and, in catastrophic cases, engine destruction. Oil dilution from DPF regeneration accelerates tensioner wear.
problems.recommendation: On any pre-2019 L494 SD4, have the timing chain inspected at 60,000 miles — a £1,200–£2,500 preventative repair is cheaper than the £4,500–£6,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare 204DTA engine prices for your Range Rover Sport →

problems.title: 508PS/306PS Supercharged V6/V8 Cooling System Failure
problems.affectedModels: L494 3.0 SCV6 (306PS) and 5.0 V8 Supercharged (508PS), all years.
problems.rootCause: Water pump failure and coolant leaks from crossover pipes are well-documented on both supercharged V6 and V8 petrols. The supercharger sits in the engine's 'V', making access to cooling components labour-intensive.
problems.recommendation: On any L494 SCV6 or V8 Supercharged approaching 60,000 miles, replace the water pump as preventative maintenance — £600–£1,600 now is cheaper than the £4,500+ engine replacement that follows an overheat.

cta.label: Compare 508PS/306PS engine prices for your Range Rover Sport →

replacementCosts.h2: L494 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are L494 Range Rover Sport only. Engine fitment varies by year — the 2016 update introduced the SD4 (204DTA) as the entry-level diesel; the 2018 facelift brought the P300 (PT204), P400e plug-in hybrid, and later the D300/D250 Ingenium six-cylinder diesels (AJ20D6/DT306). The SVR (508PS) was introduced in 2014 and continued through to the end of production.
